The film was not a box office success.[[192]](#cite_note-193) He starred in the biographical drama [*Joy*](/wiki/Joy_(2015_film) "Joy (2015 film)") (2015), opposite [Jennifer Lawrence](/wiki/Jennifer_Lawrence "Jennifer Lawrence") and Bradley Cooper, about an American inventor [Joy Mangano](/wiki/Joy_Mangano "Joy Mangano"); it gained generally mixed reviews. In 2016, he co-starred in *[Dirty Grandpa](/wiki/Dirty_Grandpa "Dirty Grandpa"),* playing a grandfather who goes to [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") during [spring break](/wiki/Spring_break "Spring break") with his grandson ([Zac Efron](/wiki/Zac_Efron "Zac Efron")). Upon release, the film received a polarized reception for its reputedly distasteful content, and appeared in several critics' lists of [worst films](/wiki/Worst_films "Worst films") of 2016.[[193]](#cite_note-194)[[194]](#cite_note-195) He also appeared in *[Hands of Stone](/wiki/Hands_of_Stone "Hands of Stone")* (2016)*,* a biographical sports drama about the career of Panamanian former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") [Roberto Durán](/wiki/Roberto_Dur%C3%A1n "Roberto Durán"). His last release of the year was [*The Comedian*](/wiki/The_Comedian_(2016_film) "The Comedian (2016 film)"), which premiered at the [AFI Fest](/wiki/AFI_Fest "AFI Fest"), a film festival celebrating filmmakers' achievements.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

### 2017–present: Resurgence and Scorsese reunion

In 2017, De Niro starred as [Bernie Madoff](/wiki/Bernie_Madoff "Bernie Madoff") in [Barry Levinson](/wiki/Barry_Levinson "Barry Levinson")'s HBO film *[The Wizard of Lies](/wiki/The_Wizard_of_Lies "The Wizard of Lies")*, a performance which earned him critical praise and a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award") nomination for [Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Television Movie](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actor_in_a_Limited_Series_or_Movie "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Movie").[[196]](#cite_note-197) In 2019, De Niro won acclaim for portraying [Robert Mueller](/wiki/Robert_Mueller "Robert Mueller") alongside [Alec Baldwin](/wiki/Alec_Baldwin "Alec Baldwin")'s [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in various episodes of *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live"),* earning him an Emmy nomination for [Outstanding Guest Actor in a Comedy Series](/wiki/Outstanding_Guest_Actor_in_a_Comedy_Series "Outstanding Guest Actor in a Comedy Series").[[197]](#cite_note-198) He received another [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") nomination for [Outstanding Limited Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Limited_or_Anthology_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Limited or Anthology Series") his work as a producer on [Ava DuVernay](/wiki/Ava_DuVernay "Ava DuVernay")'s acclaimed historical miniseries *[When They See Us](/wiki/When_They_See_Us "When They See Us")* for [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix").[[198]](#cite_note-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200)

In 2019, De Niro returned to the screen by playing talk show host Murray Franklin in [Todd Phillips](/wiki/Todd_Phillips "Todd Phillips")' psychological thriller film *[Joker](/wiki/Joker_(2019_film) "Joker (2019 film)")*, an alternative [origin story](/wiki/Origin_story "Origin story") for the [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") character the [Joker](/wiki/Joker_(character) "Joker (character)") ([Joaquin Phoenix](/wiki/Joaquin_Phoenix "Joaquin Phoenix")).[[200]](#cite_note-201) The film was a commercial success, and earned eleven nominations at the Academy Awards.[[201]](#cite_note-202) Also that year, De Niro reunited with Scorsese for *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")*, based on the 2004 book *[I Heard You Paint Houses](/wiki/I_Heard_You_Paint_Houses "I Heard You Paint Houses")* by [Charles Brandt](/wiki/Charles_Brandt "Charles Brandt").[[202]](#cite_note-203) It is their ninth feature film together and the first since 1995's *Casino*, and co-stars Al Pacino, Harvey Keitel, and Joe Pesci. The film received critical acclaim; Robbie Collin of *The Daily Telegraph,* praised De Niro's "sensational" performance and the chemistry between his co-stars, whom he has worked with in earlier films.[[203]](#cite_note-204) *Variety* magazine's critic also noted the chemistry, calling him "superb", despite perceived weaknesses in the film's [special effects](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect").[[204]](#cite_note-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/CMiks_-_The_Irishman_table_-_CCA_2020_01_12.jpg/250px-CMiks_-_The_Irishman_table_-_CCA_2020_01_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:CMiks_-_The_Irishman_table_-_CCA_2020_01_12.jpg)

De Niro (right) and [Al Pacino](/wiki/Al_Pacino "Al Pacino") during the [25th Critics' Choice Awards](/wiki/25th_Critics%27_Choice_Awards "25th Critics' Choice Awards") in January 2020

In September 2020, De Niro appeared in [Nancy Meyers](/wiki/Nancy_Meyers "Nancy Meyers")' comedy short film *[Father of the Bride Part 3(ish)](/wiki/Father_of_the_Bride_(franchise) "Father of the Bride (franchise)")*. The short co-starred [Diane Keaton](/wiki/Diane_Keaton "Diane Keaton"), [Steve Martin](/wiki/Steve_Martin "Steve Martin"), [Kieran Culkin](/wiki/Kieran_Culkin "Kieran Culkin"), [Martin Short](/wiki/Martin_Short "Martin Short") and [Florence Pugh](/wiki/Florence_Pugh "Florence Pugh").[[205]](#cite_note-206) Also in that year, De Niro appeared in *[The Comeback Trail](/wiki/The_Comeback_Trail_(2020_film) "The Comeback Trail (2020 film)")*, a crime comedy directed by [George Gallo](/wiki/George_Gallo "George Gallo").[[206]](#cite_note-207) De Niro was cast in [James Gray](/wiki/James_Gray_(director) "James Gray (director)")'s period drama *[Armageddon Time](/wiki/Armageddon_Time "Armageddon Time")*, but he dropped out of the project by the time production began.[[207]](#cite_note-208) In January 2021, De Niro signed on for the historical comedy *[Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam_(2022_film) "Amsterdam (2022 film)")*, playing an army veteran. Released in October 2022, the ensemble includes [Christian Bale](/wiki/Christian_Bale "Christian Bale"), [Margot Robbie](/wiki/Margot_Robbie "Margot Robbie"), [John David Washington](/wiki/John_David_Washington "John David Washington"), [Michael Shannon](/wiki/Michael_Shannon "Michael Shannon"), [Mike Myers](/wiki/Mike_Myers "Mike Myers"), [Timothy Olyphant](/wiki/Timothy_Olyphant "Timothy Olyphant") and [Anya Taylor-Joy](/wiki/Anya_Taylor-Joy "Anya Taylor-Joy").[[208]](#cite_note-209) The reviewer from *[South China Morning Post](/wiki/South_China_Morning_Post "South China Morning Post")* thought De Niro "brings just the right gravitas to his decorated general."[[209]](#cite_note-210) In August 2022, De Niro signed on to star in the [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") mob drama *[The Alto Knights](/wiki/The_Alto_Knights "The Alto Knights")*, directed by [Barry Levinson](/wiki/Barry_Levinson "Barry Levinson").[[210]](#cite_note-211) De Niro appeared in *[Savage Salvation](/wiki/Savage_Salvation "Savage Salvation")* as [Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriffs_in_the_United_States "Sheriffs in the United States") Church, which was released on December 2, 2022.[[211]](#cite_note-212)

In 2023, De Niro played [William King Hale](/wiki/William_King_Hale "William King Hale"), a [cattleman](/wiki/Cattleman "Cattleman") and perpetrator of the [Osage Indian murders](/wiki/Osage_Indian_murders "Osage Indian murders"), in [*Killers of the Flower Moon*](/wiki/Killers_of_the_Flower_Moon_(film) "Killers of the Flower Moon (film)"), directed by Scorsese and adapted from the book of the [same name](/wiki/Killers_of_the_Flower_Moon_(book) "Killers of the Flower Moon (book)") by [David Grann](/wiki/David_Grann "David Grann"). He starred alongside Leonardo DiCaprio and [Lily Gladstone](/wiki/Lily_Gladstone "Lily Gladstone").[[212]](#cite_note-213)[[213]](#cite_note-214) It was reported that the film's budget of $200 million had prompted Scorsese to seek [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") or [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") for production and distribution,[[214]](#cite_note-215) and in May 2020, Apple TV+ was announced to co-finance and co-distribute the film with [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures").[[215]](#cite_note-216) De Niro received an [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") nomination for the role. Niles Schulz praised the performance as "one of De Niro's best", evoking "a figure of beguiling charm, with a good humor that cloaks bottomless indifference."[[216]](#cite_note-217)

In 2023, De Niro also appeared in the comedy *[About My Father](/wiki/About_My_Father "About My Father")*,[[217]](#cite_note-218) and in the television series *Nada.*[[218]](#cite_note-219) On March 1, 2023, it was announced that De Niro will produce and star in the six-episode limited series *[Zero Day](/wiki/Zero_Day_(American_TV_series) "Zero Day (American TV series)")* for Netflix, a conspiracy thriller created by [Eric Newman](/wiki/Eric_Newman_(producer) "Eric Newman (producer)") and [Noah Oppenheim](/wiki/Noah_Oppenheim "Noah Oppenheim"), who will also executive produce along with [Jonathan Glickman](/wiki/Jonathan_Glickman "Jonathan Glickman").[[219]](#cite_note-220) The series premiered in 2025.[[220]](#cite_note-221) Another film he starred in, *[Ezra](/wiki/Ezra_(2023_film) "Ezra (2023 film)")*, made its premiere at [Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival") 2023 and was acquired by [Bleecker Street](/wiki/Bleecker_Street_(company) "Bleecker Street (company)") for theatrical release in 2024. De Niro said he took interest in the film as it features the complexities of parenting an autistic child; De Niro has an autistic son himself.[[221]](#cite_note-222)

De Niro was due to receive a leadership award from the [National Association of Broadcasters](/wiki/National_Association_of_Broadcasters "National Association of Broadcasters") in 2024, before the honour was withdrawn following De Niro's criticism of Donald Trump outside [the former President's criminal trial in New York](/wiki/Prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_in_New_York "Prosecution of Donald Trump in New York").[[222]](#cite_note-223)

## Reception and legacy

Several journalists consider De Niro to be among the best actors of his generation.[[2]](#cite_note-Baron-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Pulver-3)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantelliSantelli2010section_95-224)[[4]](#cite_note-Wells-4) John Naughton of *[GQ](/wiki/GQ "GQ")* magazine believes that De Niro has "redefined what we can expect of an actor."[[5]](#cite_note-Naughton-5) [A. O. Scott](/wiki/A._O._Scott "A. O. Scott") said that De Niro "was transforming himself – physically, vocally, psychologically – with each new role. And in the process, before our eyes, reinventing the art of acting."[[224]](#cite_note-225) As early as 1977, *Newsweek* remarked that the actor "gives you the shock of becoming, of a metamorphosis that can be thrilling, moving, or frightening."[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrode199312-226) Biographer Douglas Brode praises De Niro's versatility and ability to inhabit any role, although [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") once said in 1983 that she did not like how the actor was "disfiguring" himself in films such as *Raging Bull*.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrode19938-227) When asked why he undertook such roles, De Niro responded, "To totally submerge into another character and experience life through him, without having to risk the real-life consequences—well it's a cheap way to do doing things that you would never dare to do yourself."[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrode199312-226)

In 2009, he was announced as one of the [Kennedy Center Honorees](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors") with the commemoration: "One of America's greatest cinematic actors, Robert De Niro has demonstrated a legendary commitment to his characters and has co-founded one of the world's major film festivals."[[227]](#cite_note-228) [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and [Meryl Streep](/wiki/Meryl_Streep "Meryl Streep") honored him at the event. In 2016, he received the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") from President Barack Obama.[[228]](#cite_note-229) Obama said "Everybody on this stage has touched me in a very powerful, very personal way [...] These are folks who have helped make me who I am."[[229]](#cite_note-time.com-230) [White House Press Secretary](/wiki/White_House_Press_Secretary "White House Press Secretary") [Josh Earnest](/wiki/Josh_Earnest "Josh Earnest") added, "There is no arguing that the individuals who will be honored today are richly deserving", he said.[[229]](#cite_note-time.com-230)

Many of De Niro's films have become classics of American cinema, in which six of them have been inducted into the [U.S. National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") as of 2022.[[6]](#cite_note-Film_Registry-7) Five films are featured on the American Film Institute's (AFI) list of the [100 greatest American films](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Movies "AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies") of all time. De Niro and [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart") share the title for the most films represented on the AFI list.[[230]](#cite_note-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232) In 2006, De Niro donated his collection of film-related materials, such as scripts, wardrobe pieces and [props](/wiki/Props "Props"), to the [Harry Ransom Center](/wiki/Harry_Ransom_Center "Harry Ransom Center") at the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin").[[232]](#cite_note-233) The collection, which took more than two years to process and catalog, opened to the public in 2009.[[233]](#cite_note-234) *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* magazine's list of 100 best movies included seven of De Niro's films, as chosen by actors in the industry.[[234]](#cite_note-Time_Out-235) In May 2025, De Niro received the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival")'s [Honorary Palme d'Or](/wiki/Honorary_Palme_d%27Or "Honorary Palme d'Or").[[235]](#cite_note-236)

**Fan song**  
In 1984, the English girl group [Bananarama](/wiki/Bananarama "Bananarama")'s fan song "[Robert De Niro's Waiting...](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro%27s_Waiting... "Robert De Niro's Waiting...")" reached the third place of the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart"), remaining on the charts for 12 weeks.[[236]](#cite_note-237)

**Honors**

* 1993 – [Venice Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival")'s [Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement](/wiki/Golden_Lion_for_Lifetime_Achievement "Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement")
* 1997 – [Moscow International Film Festival](/wiki/Moscow_International_Film_Festival "Moscow International Film Festival") Honorable Prize
* 2001 – [Gotham Award](/wiki/Gotham_Award "Gotham Award") Lifetime Achievement Award
* 2003 – [AFI Life Achievement Award](/wiki/AFI_Life_Achievement_Award "AFI Life Achievement Award") and tribute
* 2009 – [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors")
* 2011 – [Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Cecil_B._DeMille_Award "Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award")
* 2013 – [Santa Barbara International Film Festival](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_International_Film_Festival "Santa Barbara International Film Festival") Kirk Douglas Award
* 2016 – Presidential Medal of Freedom
* 2017 – [Film Society of Lincoln Center](/wiki/Film_Society_of_Lincoln_Center "Film Society of Lincoln Center") Gala Tribute

* 2020 – [Screen Actors Guild Life Achievement Award](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Life_Achievement_Award "Screen Actors Guild Life Achievement Award")
* 2025 — [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival")'s [Honorary Palme d'Or](/wiki/Honorary_Palme_d%27Or "Honorary Palme d'Or")

## Business interests

In 1989, De Niro and partner Jane Rosenthal co-founded the film production company TriBeCa Productions, which also organizes the Tribeca Film Festival. De Niro owns [Tribeca Grill](/wiki/Tribeca_Grill "Tribeca Grill") (co-owned with Broadway producer [Stewart F. Lane](/wiki/Stewart_F._Lane "Stewart F. Lane")), a [New American](/wiki/New_American_cuisine "New American cuisine") restaurant located at 375 Greenwich Street (at Franklin Street) in Tribeca, Manhattan.[[237]](#cite_note-238) It opened in 1990.[[238]](#cite_note-239) He is also the owner of the Greenwich Hotel in Tribeca.[[239]](#cite_note-240) De Niro co-owns [Nobu](/wiki/Nobu "Nobu") restaurants and hotels with partners Meir Teper and Chef [Nobu Matsuhisa](/wiki/Nobu_Matsuhisa "Nobu Matsuhisa"). The first Nobu Hotel opened inside [Caesars Palace](/wiki/Caesars_Palace "Caesars Palace"), Las Vegas, in 2013. Two years later, the second Nobu Hotel opened at [City of Dreams](/wiki/City_of_Dreams_Manila "City of Dreams Manila") in Manila, Philippines. In 2015, De Niro partnered with [James Packer](/wiki/James_Packer "James Packer") after the billionaire acquired a 20 percent stake in Nobu for $100 million.[[240]](#cite_note-241) He is a stakeholder in Paradise Found Nobu Resort, a company planning to build a luxury resort on the island of [Barbuda](/wiki/Barbuda "Barbuda").[[241]](#cite_note-242)[[242]](#cite_note-243)[[243]](#cite_note-244)
In 2025, De Niro appeared alongside Al Pacino in Moncler's "Warmer Together" fashion campaign.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

## Personal life

De Niro is a long-term resident of New York City, and has been investing in Manhattan's [Tribeca](/wiki/Tribeca "Tribeca") neighborhood since 1989. He has properties on the [east](/wiki/East_Side_(Manhattan) "East Side (Manhattan)") and [west](/wiki/West_Side_(Manhattan) "West Side (Manhattan)") sides of Manhattan. He also has a 78-acre (32-hectare) estate in [Gardiner](/wiki/Gardiner,_New_York "Gardiner, New York"), New York, which serves as his primary residence.[[245]](#cite_note-Gardiner_lawsuit-246)

In 1998, De Niro lobbied [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") against [impeaching](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Bill_Clinton "Impeachment of Bill Clinton") President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton").[[246]](#cite_note-247)

In October 2003, De Niro was diagnosed with [prostate cancer](/wiki/Prostate_cancer "Prostate cancer"). He underwent surgery at the [Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center](/wiki/Memorial_Sloan-Kettering_Cancer_Center "Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center") in December 2003.[[247]](#cite_note-248)

In 2006, De Niro received Italian citizenship, despite opposition by the [Sons of Italy](/wiki/Sons_of_Italy "Sons of Italy"), who believe that De Niro damaged the public image of Italians by portraying criminals.[[248]](#cite_note-web.archive.org-249)[[249]](#cite_note-femalefirst.co.uk-250)

In 2012, De Niro joined the anti-[fracking](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking") campaign [Artists Against Fracking](/wiki/Artists_Against_Fracking "Artists Against Fracking").[[250]](#cite_note-251)

In 2013, De Niro attended [Israeli President](/wiki/Israeli_President "Israeli President") [Shimon Peres](/wiki/Shimon_Peres "Shimon Peres")'s 90th birthday celebration, as well as the [Israeli Presidential conference](/wiki/Israeli_Presidential_Conference "Israeli Presidential Conference"). He praised Peres as "a great person, [and] a great statesman."[[251]](#cite_note-252)[[252]](#cite_note-253)[[253]](#cite_note-254)

In 2016, De Niro initially defended the inclusion of a controversial documentary, *[Vaxxed](/wiki/Vaxxed "Vaxxed")*, at the 2016 Tribeca Film Festival; the film was directed and co-written by discredited [anti-vaccine activist](/wiki/Anti-vaccine_activist "Anti-vaccine activist") [Andrew Wakefield](/wiki/Andrew_Wakefield "Andrew Wakefield") and echoed Wakefield's fraudulent claim that there was a causal link between the [MMR vaccine and autism](/wiki/MMR_vaccine_and_autism "MMR vaccine and autism").[[254]](#cite_note-255)[[255]](#cite_note-Guardian2016-256)[[256]](#cite_note-HRoffit-257) De Niro explained that his interest in the film was from his personal experience with his [autistic](/wiki/Autistic "Autistic") son, Elliot.[[255]](#cite_note-Guardian2016-256) The film was withdrawn from the schedule after consultation with the festival organizers and scientific community.[[255]](#cite_note-Guardian2016-256)[[257]](#cite_note-258) During a subsequent appearance on [NBC's](/wiki/NBC%27s "NBC's") *[Today](/wiki/Today_(American_TV_program) "Today (American TV program)")*, De Niro expressed regret that he agreed to exclude *Vaxxed* from the festival and stated, "I think the movie is something that people should see." Also during the appearance, De Niro promoted the 2014 anti-vaccination film *[Trace Amounts](/wiki/Trace_Amounts "Trace Amounts")* and stated that he doubted the scientific consensus that there was no link between [autism and vaccines](/wiki/Autism_and_vaccines "Autism and vaccines").[[258]](#cite_note-259)[[259]](#cite_note-260) In February 2017, De Niro took part in a joint presentation with [Robert F. Kennedy Jr.](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Jr. "Robert F. Kennedy Jr."), chairman of the anti-vaccine non-profit [Children's Health Defense](/wiki/Children%27s_Health_Defense "Children's Health Defense"), to discuss their concerns with vaccine safety. De Niro has stated that he is not anti-vaccination, but does question their efficacy.[[260]](#cite_note-261)

Later in 2016, De Niro attended an annual celebrity gala for the [Friends of the Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Friends_of_the_Israel_Defense_Forces "Friends of the Israel Defense Forces") at the [Beverly Hilton](/wiki/Beverly_Hilton "Beverly Hilton") in Los Angeles. The event raised $38 million for the organization.[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-263)[[263]](#cite_note-264)

In October 2018, De Niro was [targeted by an explosive device](/wiki/October_2018_United_States_mail_bombing_attempts "October 2018 United States mail bombing attempts"). The device was found at the Tribeca Grill, which also houses his production company in Manhattan. According to the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI"), similar devices were sent to high-profile politicians including [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"), [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), former Attorney General [Eric Holder](/wiki/Eric_Holder "Eric Holder"), and former [CIA Director](/wiki/CIA_Director "CIA Director") [John Brennan](/wiki/John_Brennan_(CIA_officer) "John Brennan (CIA officer)").[[264]](#cite_note-265)[[265]](#cite_note-266)

De Niro was a supporter of President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") for re-election in [2024](/wiki/United_States_presidential_election,_2024 "United States presidential election, 2024"),[[266]](#cite_note-267) and backed Vice President [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris")'s [campaign](/wiki/Kamala_Harris_presidential_campaign,_2024 "Kamala Harris presidential campaign, 2024") after Biden dropped out of the race.[[267]](#cite_note-268) He had previously criticized President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), calling him a "buffoon", believing that he could become a "vicious dictator" and [comparing his politics to fascism](/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_fascism "Donald Trump and fascism").[[268]](#cite_note-269)[[269]](#cite_note-270)

### Relationships

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Robert_De_Niro_Grace_Hightower_VF_2012_Shankbone_2.JPG/250px-Robert_De_Niro_Grace_Hightower_VF_2012_Shankbone_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Robert_De_Niro_Grace_Hightower_VF_2012_Shankbone_2.JPG)

De Niro with [Grace Hightower](/wiki/Grace_Hightower "Grace Hightower") in April 2012

De Niro married actress [Diahnne Abbott](/wiki/Diahnne_Abbott "Diahnne Abbott") in 1976. They have a son, [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael_De_Niro "Raphael De Niro"), a former actor who works in New York real estate.[[270]](#cite_note-271) De Niro also adopted Abbott's daughter [Drena](/wiki/Drena_De_Niro "Drena De Niro") from a previous relationship. He and Abbott divorced in 1988.

De Niro was in a relationship with model [Toukie Smith](/wiki/Toukie_Smith "Toukie Smith") from 1988 to 1996. The couple has twins, son Julian and daughter Airyn, conceived by [*in vitro* fertilization](/wiki/In_vitro_fertilization "In vitro fertilization") and delivered by a [surrogate](/wiki/Surrogacy "Surrogacy") mother in 1995.[[271]](#cite_note-people-dec2011-272)[[272]](#cite_note-273) Airyn [came out](/wiki/Came_out "Came out") as a [trans woman](/wiki/Trans_woman "Trans woman") in April 2025,[[273]](#cite_note-274) and Robert stated that he supported her identity.[[274]](#cite_note-275)

In 1997, De Niro married actress [Grace Hightower](/wiki/Grace_Hightower "Grace Hightower").[[275]](#cite_note-Custody-276) Their son, Elliot, was born in 1998 and the couple split in 1999. The divorce was never finalized and in 2004 they renewed their vows.[[275]](#cite_note-Custody-276) In December 2011, their daughter Helen was born via surrogate.[[276]](#cite_note-277) In 2014, he and Hightower moved into a 6,000-square-foot, five-bedroom apartment at [15 Central Park West](/wiki/15_Central_Park_West "15 Central Park West").[[277]](#cite_note-278)[[278]](#cite_note-279) Four years later, De Niro and Hightower separated after 20 years of marriage.[[279]](#cite_note-280) De Niro has four grandchildren: one from his daughter Drena and three from his son Raphael.[[280]](#cite_note-281)[[281]](#cite_note-282) On April 19, 2021, De Niro's lawyer argued in a virtual divorce hearing presided by a Manhattan judge that he is "working at an unsustainable pace" in order "to support Hightower and pay off all his back taxes." Hightower's lawyer claimed that since the pair filed for divorce in 2018, De Niro had been "unfairly decreasing" the agreed-upon payments to her.[[282]](#cite_note-virtual-283)

In April 2023, De Niro welcomed his seventh child, a daughter Gia, with his girlfriend Tiffany Chen.[[283]](#cite_note-284)[[284]](#cite_note-285)[[285]](#cite_note-286)[[286]](#cite_note-287) At age 79, De Niro is one of the [oldest fathers on record](/wiki/List_of_oldest_fathers "List of oldest fathers").[[287]](#cite_note-288)[[288]](#cite_note-289)

In July 2023, it was announced that De Niro's grandson through his daughter Drena, Leandro De Niro Rodriguez, had died at age 19.[[289]](#cite_note-Leo'sdeath-290) The cause of death was determined as a [combined drug intoxication](/wiki/Combined_drug_intoxication "Combined drug intoxication") involving [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl") and [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine").[[290]](#cite_note-291)

### Legal issues

In February 1998, De Niro was held for questioning by French police in connection with an international prostitution ring.[[291]](#cite_note-292) De Niro denied any involvement,[[292]](#cite_note-bbcfurious-293) and later filed a complaint against the [examining magistrate](/wiki/Examining_magistrate "Examining magistrate") for "violation of secrecy in an investigation".[[293]](#cite_note-294)[[294]](#cite_note-295) He stated he would not return to France, but has since traveled there several times including for the [2011 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/2011_Cannes_Film_Festival "2011 Cannes Film Festival").[[295]](#cite_note-296)

In 1999, De Niro threatened to sue the owners of "De Niro's Supper Club" in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), under section 3 of the [BC Privacy Act](/wiki/Freedom_of_Information_and_Protection_of_Privacy_Act_(British_Columbia) "Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (British Columbia)").[[296]](#cite_note-297) The restaurant subsequently changed its name to "Section (3)".[[297]](#cite_note-298)

In 2006, the [trust](/wiki/Land_trust "Land trust") that owns De Niro's Gardiner estate sued the town to have its [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") [assessment](/wiki/Tax_assessment "Tax assessment") reduced, arguing that $6 million was too high and should be compared only with similar properties in [Ulster County](/wiki/Ulster_County "Ulster County"), where Gardiner is located. The town, which had been comparing its value to similar estates in [Dutchess County](/wiki/Dutchess_County "Dutchess County"), across the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), and [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut")'s [Litchfield County](/wiki/Litchfield_County "Litchfield County"), where many other affluent New York residents maintain estates on large properties, won in [State Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_State_Supreme_Court "New York State Supreme Court").[[298]](#cite_note-NY_Times_Gardiner_story-299) In 2014, the trust's lawyers appealed the decision and the town was unsure if it should continue to defend the suit because of financial limitations (it would have earned far less in payments on the increased taxes than it had spent on legal costs). This angered many residents, who initially sympathized with De Niro, and some proposed to raise money privately to help the town continue the suit.[[245]](#cite_note-Gardiner_lawsuit-246) The dispute was publicized by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*. "When he (De Niro) read about it on [Election Day](/wiki/Election_Day_(United_States) "Election Day (United States)"), he went bananas", due to the negative publicity, said Gardiner town councilman Warren Wiegand.[[299]](#cite_note-Gardiner_settlement-300) He was unaware that a lawsuit was filed; the trust's accountants took responsibility citing [fiduciary duty](/wiki/Fiduciary_duty "Fiduciary duty").[[299]](#cite_note-Gardiner_settlement-300) Shortly afterwards, De Niro's lawyer, Tom Harvey, contacted the town to settle the case, and the trust's accounting firm agreed to pay the town's $129,000 legal bill.[[300]](#cite_note-NYT_Gardiner_settlement_story-301)

In August 2019, De Niro's company Canal Productions filed a $6-million lawsuit against former employee Graham Chase Robinson, for breaching her fiduciary duties and violating New York's [faithless servant](/wiki/Faithless_servant "Faithless servant") doctrine by misusing company funds and watching hours of [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") during work hours.[[301]](#cite_note-302)[[302]](#cite_note-303) In October 2019, Robinson filed a lawsuit against De Niro, claiming harassment and [gender discrimination](/wiki/Gender_discrimination "Gender discrimination").[[303]](#cite_note-HarassmentLawsuit-304) In November 2023 the jury found De Niro not personally liable for gender discrimination but his production company was ordered to pay her $1.2 million in damages.[[304]](#cite_note-305)

## Filmography and accolades

Main articles: [Robert De Niro filmography](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro_filmography "Robert De Niro filmography") and [List of awards and nominations received by Robert De Niro](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Robert_De_Niro "List of awards and nominations received by Robert De Niro")

Prolific in film since the 1970s, De Niro's most critically acclaimed films, according to the review aggregate site [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), include [*Bang the Drum Slowly*](/wiki/Bang_the_Drum_Slowly_(film) "Bang the Drum Slowly (film)") (1973), *[Mean Streets](/wiki/Mean_Streets "Mean Streets")* (1973), *[The Godfather Part II](/wiki/The_Godfather_Part_II "The Godfather Part II")* (1974), *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* (1976), *[The Deer Hunter](/wiki/The_Deer_Hunter "The Deer Hunter")* (1978), *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[The King of Comedy](/wiki/The_King_of_Comedy_(film) "The King of Comedy (film)")* (1983), *[Once Upon a Time in America](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_America "Once Upon a Time in America")* (1984), *[Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_(1985_film) "Brazil (1985 film)")* (1985), *[The Mission](/wiki/The_Mission_(1986_film) "The Mission (1986 film)")* (1986), *[Midnight Run](/wiki/Midnight_Run "Midnight Run")* (1988), *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995), *[Heat](/wiki/Heat_(1995_film) "Heat (1995 film)")* (1995), *[Meet the Parents](/wiki/Meet_the_Parents "Meet the Parents")* (2000), *[Silver Linings Playbook](/wiki/Silver_Linings_Playbook "Silver Linings Playbook")* (2012), *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")* (2019), and *[Killers of the Flower Moon](/wiki/Killers_of_the_Flower_Moon_(film) "Killers of the Flower Moon (film)")* (2023).[[305]](#cite_note-306)

De Niro has been recognized by the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences") for the following performances:[[306]](#cite_note-307)

* [47th Academy Awards](/wiki/47th_Academy_Awards "47th Academy Awards") (1974): [Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor"), **win**, for *The Godfather Part II*
* [49th Academy Awards](/wiki/49th_Academy_Awards "49th Academy Awards") (1976): [Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor"), nomination, for *Taxi Driver*
* [51st Academy Awards](/wiki/51st_Academy_Awards "51st Academy Awards") (1978): Best Actor, nomination, for *The Deer Hunter*
* [53rd Academy Awards](/wiki/53rd_Academy_Awards "53rd Academy Awards") (1980): Best Actor, **win**, for *Raging Bull*
* [63rd Academy Awards](/wiki/63rd_Academy_Awards "63rd Academy Awards") (1990): Best Actor, nomination, for *[Awakenings](/wiki/Awakenings "Awakenings")*
* [64th Academy Awards](/wiki/64th_Academy_Awards "64th Academy Awards") (1991): Best Actor, nomination, for *[Cape Fear](/wiki/Cape_Fear_(1991_film) "Cape Fear (1991 film)")*
* [85th Academy Awards](/wiki/85th_Academy_Awards "85th Academy Awards") (2012): Best Supporting Actor, nomination, for *Silver Linings Playbook*
* [92nd Academy Awards](/wiki/92nd_Academy_Awards "92nd Academy Awards") (2019): [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture"), nomination, for *The Irishman*
* [96th Academy Awards](/wiki/96th_Academy_Awards "96th Academy Awards") (2023): Best Supporting Actor, nomination, for *Killers of the Flower Moon*

De Niro has won two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"): [Best Actor – Motion Picture Drama](/wiki/Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Drama "Best Actor – Motion Picture Drama") for *Raging Bull* and a [Cecil B. DeMille Award](/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille_Award "Cecil B. DeMille Award") for "outstanding contributions to the world of entertainment."[[307]](#cite_note-308) He was also the 56th recipient of [Screen Actors Guild Life Achievement Award](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Life_Achievement_Award "Screen Actors Guild Life Achievement Award"); [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio"), who co-starred with De Niro in *This Boy's Life*, presented him the award, citing him as an inspiration and influence.[[308]](#cite_note-309)[[309]](#cite_note-310)

On May 13, 2025, De Niro received the [Honorary Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival"), celebrating his five-decade career in cinema.[[310]](#cite_note-311)[[311]](#cite_note-312)
